 README Document for GRACE Data Assimilation Version 3.0 Global Product

1.0 Introduction 

This document provides basic information for using the Gravity Recovery and Climate 
Experiment (GRACE) Data Assimilation for Drought Monitoring Global Version 3.0 product 
(abbreviated as GRACE-DA-DM GL V3.0).  

Scientists at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center generate groundwater and soil moisture 
drought indicators each week. These indicators are based on terrestrial water storage 
observations derived from the GRACE and GRACE-FO satellite data and integrated with other 
observations, using a sophisticated numerical model of land surface water and energy 
processes. The data product of Groundwater and Soil Moisture Conditions from GRACE Data 
Assimilation contains a set of drought indicators for the North American Drought Monitor. 

The GRACE-DA-DM is an operational project which produces groundwater and soil moisture 
drought indicators each week.  The operational data is available from https://nasagrace.unl.edu 
weekly with a 2-9 day latency.  The GRACE-DA-DM data distributed here at GESDISC is the final 
archive version, which is generated after the latest GRACE-FO data are available, with 3-6 
monthly latency. 

1.1  Dataset Basic Characteristics  

The GRACE-DA-DM GL V3.0 data product contains three drought indicators: Groundwater 
Percentile, Root Zone Soil Moisture Percentile, and Surface Soil Moisture Percentile. These 
drought indicators express wet or dry conditions as a percentile, indicating the probability of 
occurrence within the period of record 1948 to 2014. The drought indicator data are daily, but 
available only one day (Monday) per week. The data have a spatial resolution of 0.25 x 0.25 
degree with global coverage (60S, 180W, 90N, 180E) and a temporal range from February 2003 
to present (with a 3-6 months latency). The data are archived in netCDF format.

1.2 Digital Object Identifier (DOI) and Citation 

A Digital Object Identifier, or DOI, is a unique alphanumeric string used to identify a  digital 
object and provide a permanent link online.  DOIs are often used in online  publications in 
citations.  

DOI for GRACE-DA-DM GL V3.0 data product: 10.5067/SZYMDFZ7TJ4H 

When using this dataset, please cite both the data and the primary reference for the data, as 
shown below. 

Data Citation: Matthew Rodell, Bailing Li, A. Getirana, and Hiroko Kato Beaudoing, 
NASA/GSFC/HSL (2022), Groundwater and Soil Moisture Conditions from GRACE and GRACE-FO 
Data Assimilation L4 7-days 0.25 x 0.25 degree Global Version 3.0, Greenbelt, Maryland, USA: 
Goddard Earth Sciences Data and Information Services Center (GES DISC), Accessed [Data 
Access Date] 10.5067/SZYMDFZ7TJ4H 

Primary Reference: 
Li, B., M. Rodell, S.V. Kumar, H.K. Beaudoing, A. Getirana, B.F. Zaitchik, L.G. Goncalves, C. Cossetin, S. 
Bhanja, A. Mukherjee, S. Tian, N. Tangdamrongsub, D. Long, J. Nanteza, J. Lee, F. Policelli, I. B. Goni, D. 
Daira, M. Bila, G. de Lannoy, D. Mocko, and S. C. Steele-Dunne, 2019: Global GRACE data assimilation for 
groundwater and drought monitoring: advances and challenges, Water Resour. Res., 55, 
doi:10.1029/2018WR024618.

1.4 What’s New? 

1.4.1 What’s in GRACE-DA-DM GLV3.0?  

The GRACE-DA-DM GL V3.0 is based on the Catchment Land Surface Model (CLSM) Fortuna 2.5 version 
simulation that was created within the Land Information System data assimilation framework (Kumar et 
al., 2016). GRACE-DA-DM GL V3.0 drought indicator maps are derived from the 
GLDAS_CLSM025_DA1_D product, at 0.25 degree resolution, forced by European Centre for Medium-
Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) meteorological data, and assimilated RL06 GRACE and GRACE Follow 
On (GRACE-FO) data from the University of Texas at Austin (Save et al., 2016; Save, 2020). The 
GRACE/GRACE-FO versions were assimilated at the time of simulation which were restarted from the 
last available GRACE data month and extended through present for each week. 

The GRACE-DA-DM GL V3.0 is similar to the GRACE-DA-DM U.S. V4.0 product. Both products are based 
on the Catchment Land Surface Model (CLSM) Fortuna 2.5 version simulation that was created within 

7 

 
 
 
 
 README Document for GRACE Data Assimilation Version 3.0 Global Product 

the Land Information System data assimilation framework (Kumar et al., 2016). Also, both products 
applied the 7-day moving window climatology in the percentile computation. However, the Global and 
US simulations are forced differently and have different temporal and spatial resolutions. This means 
the TWS fields from the GL and US simulations in an overlapping area may differ as a result of the GRACE 
data version, but also due to the different meteorological datasets and resolution. 

1.4.2 Revision history of GRACE-DA-DM    

The GRACE-DA-DM Version 1.0 was created by the stand alone CLSM (an older version) using 
the GRACE-Tellus 1 degree data from the Center for Space Research at University of Texas. The 
GRACE data assimilation (DA) is executed on a grid-to-grid basis in GRACE-DA_DM Version 2.0, 
while a basin scale average was used in Version 1.0 (Zaitchik et al. 2008). The Version 2.0 data 
were reprocessed (on June 14, 2017), using the GRACE RL05 Mascon solutions version 1 data 
set from UT CSR, for the entire period from April 1, 2002 to June 5, 2017. The reprocessing 
included fixes in the DA and increased the bedrock depth by 3 meters (US) and 2 meters 
(Global) to enhance the drought indicator calculations.  

The GRACE-DA-DM Version GL 3.0 used the GRACE RL06 (GRACE; 2002-2017) and GRACE Follow 
On (GRACE-FO; 2018-present) Mascon solutions, at 0.25 degree resolution, from the University 
of Texas at Austin (Save et al. 2016; Save 2020).  This Global simulation assimilated the versions 
of GRACE/GRACE-FO data at the time of simulation which were restarted from the last available 
GRACE data month and extended through present for each week, using the baseline of 2004-
2018 mean for Terrestrial Water Storage anomaly computation. GRACE-DA-DM GL Version 3.0 
uses both version 1 and version 2 RL06 GRACE/GRACE-FO data. The RL06 GRACE/GRACE-FO 
version 1 data, covering April 2002 to August 2019, was released in October 2019 then went 
through incremental changes each time the GRACE-FO observation was extended monthly, and 
arrived at the stable version 2 in August 2020 (covering thru 2020/06).  The percentile 
computation was switched to a 7-day moving average climatology instead of monthly, to 
improve the temporal transition of drought/wetness conditions.   

The GRACE-DA-DM U.S. Version 4.0 used the latest version 2 of the RL06 GRACE/GRACE-FO 
Mascon solutions, at 0.25 degree resolution, from the University of Texas at Austin (Save et al. 
2016; Save 2020).  The CLSM soil parameters were updated to address a soil moisture dry limit 
issue found near Zapata, Texas and in the data assimilation, the baseline for Terrestrial Water 
Storage anomaly computation was updated to 2003-2019 mean whereas previous simulations 
used 2003-2016 mean. The percentile computation is based on a 7-day moving average 
climatology.  The CLSM simulation was reprocessed using the version 2 GRACE/GRACE-FO data 
for the entire 2002/04-2020/06 period and uses the same configuration as the Version 2.0 for 
the DA scheme and increased bedrock depth, with the updates previously mentioned, for the 
US domain. Thus, Global and U.S. products have different version numbers currently.  The latest 
GRACE-DA-DM version for respective regions supersede the previous versions. 

1.5 Quality Issues 

8 

 
 README Document for GRACE Data Assimilation Version 3.0 Global Product 

Users should be aware when comparing GRACE-DA-DM datasets for analyses as different 
meteorological forcing datasets may present different biases, and data assimilation exerts 
changes in the modeled states.  Possible discontinuities related to changes in forcing dataset 
may exist over a region or a period, such as changes in the in-situ network used in the dataset. 

Due to unreliable Greenland forcing data and the lack of a glacier/ice sheet model, snow water 
equivalent accumulates indefinitely in Greenland, a few other Arctic points, and permafrost 
regions in the Himalayas.  Therefore, it is highly recommended that Greenland and other points 
with abnormally large snow water equivalent values be masked out when performing global 
analyses.  

There are many factors that influence the accuracy of GRACE-DA-DM estimates.  These include 
(1) uncertainty in the meteorological forcing data, which tends to be larger at high latitudes, 
high altitudes, and in regions with sparse observational networks, (2) simplified representations 
of complex physical processes in the models, and (3) errors in the land surface parameters that 
describe vegetation and land cover, soil properties, and elevation, and (4) inadequate 
calibration of model parameters.  

2.0 Data Organization 

2.1 File Naming Convention 

GRACE-DA-DM data files are named in accordance with the following convention: 
GRACEDADM_<Model><Grid spacing><Region>_<Temporal spacing>.A<Date>.<Product 
version>.nc4 

Attribute 
GRACEDADM 
<Model> 
<Grid spacing> 
<Region> 
<Temporal spacing> 
<Date> 
<Product version> 
Filename example: GRACEDADM_CLSM025GL_7D.A20020401.030.nc4 

Description 
GRACE Data Assimilation for Drought Monitoring 
“CLSM” for the Catchment Land Surface Model 
“025” for 1/4th (0.25) degree 
“GL” for Global 
“7D” for 7-days  
<YYYYMMDD>  
 “030” for Version 3.0 

2.2 File Format and Structure 

9 

 
 
 
 
 README Document for GRACE Data Assimilation Version 3.0 Global Product 

The GRACE-DA-DM GL V3.0 files are archived in netCDF format, which is a set of software libraries 
and self-describing, machine-independent data formats that support the creation, access, and 
sharing of array-oriented scientific data, https://www.unidata.ucar.edu/software/netcdf/docs/.  

3.0 Data Content 

3.1 Products/Parameters 

The GRACE-DA-DM GL V3.0 data product contains three drought indicators: Groundwater 
Percentile, Root Zone Soil Moisture Percentile, and Surface Soil Moisture Percentile. The 
drought indicators express wet or dry conditions as a percentile, indicating the probability of 
occurrence within the period of record from 1948 to 2014. The combination of the GRACE-
assimilated drought indicators, the 7-day moving average climatology, and the updated 
Catchment Model Parameters should provide a comprehensive and objective identification of 
drought conditions.  More information is available at https://nasagrace.unl.edu/.  

Table 2. Variables in the GRACE-DA-DM Data Product 

Short Name 
gws_inst 
rtzsm_inst 
sfsm_inst 

Description 
Groundwater Storage Percentile 
Root Zone Soil Moisture Percentile 
Surface Soil Moisture Percentile 

Unit 
%  
%  
% 

4.0 Options for Reading the Data 

4.1 Utilities 

GRACE-DA-DM data are archived in the self-describing and machine-independent netCDF 
format.  A Unidata page (https://www.unidata.ucar.edu/software/netcdf/software.html) 
provides a list of software for manipulating or displaying netCDF Data. 

4.2 Panoply 

10 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 README Document for GRACE Data Assimilation Version 3.0 Global Product 

Panoply (https://www.giss.nasa.gov/tools/panoply/) is a cross-platform application that plots 
geo-referenced and other arrays from netCDF, HDF, GRIB, and other data formats. The HowTo 
section of NASA GES DISC provides a recipe for Quick View Data with Panoply.  

5.0 Data Services 

5.1 NASA Earthdata Login System  

As of August 1, 2016, access to GES DISC data requires all users to be registered with the 
Earthdata Login system. Data continue to be free of charge and accessible via HTTPS. As of 
October 3, 2016, access to data via FTP is no longer available. Detailed instructions on how to 
register and receive authorization to access GES DISC data are provided at 
https://disc.gsfc.nasa.gov/data-access. 

GES DISC users who deploy scripting methods to list and download data in bulk via anonymous 
FTP are advised to review the How to Download Data Files from HTTP Service with wget recipe 
that provides examples of GNU wget commands for listing and downloading data via HTTPS. 

If you need assistance or wish to report a problem:  
Email: gsfc-dl-help-disc@mail.nasa.gov 
Voice: 301-614-5224  
Fax: 301-614-5268  
Address:  

Goddard Earth Sciences Data and Information Services Center  
NASA Goddard Space Flight Center  
Code 610.2  
Greenbelt, MD 20771 USA 

5.2 Data Services   

The GRACE-DA-DM GL V3.0 data product landing page provides the product summary, data 
citation, documentation, and data access methods. 

5.2.1 HTTPS  

Access the online archive data via HTTPS: 
https://hydro1.gesdisc.eosdis.nasa.gov/data/GRACEDA/GRACEDADM_CLSM025GL_7D.3.0.    

5.2.2 Earthdata Search 

11 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 README Document for GRACE Data Assimilation Version 3.0 Global Product 

Use Earthdata Search to find and retrieve data sets across multiple data centers: 
https://search.earthdata.nasa.gov/search?q=GRACEDADM_CLSM025GL_7D. 

5.2.3 OPeNDAP 

Access the data via the OPeNDAP protocol for parameter and spatial subsetting:   
https://hydro1.gesdisc.eosdis.nasa.gov/opendap/GRACEDA/GRACEDADM_CLSM025GL_7D.3.0/   

5.2.4 Giovanni 

The GES-DISC Interactive Online Visualization ANd aNalysis Interface (Giovanni) is a web-based 
tool that allows users to interactively visualize and analyze data: 
https://giovanni.gsfc.nasa.gov/giovanni/ - dataKeyword=GRACEDADM_CLSM025GL_7D_3.0 

5.2.5 THREDDS (TDS) 

The THREDDS Data Server for this mission provides data access to aggregated datasets through 
OPeNDAP, WCS, WMS, and various others. The datasets are aggregated to one file, where the 
spatial extent and variables can be subsetted and retrieved.  

https://hydro1.gesdisc.eosdis.nasa.gov/thredds/catalog/GRACEDA_aggregation/GRACEDADM_
CLSM025GL_7D.3.0/catalog.html  

There are published How-To articles to walk users through accessing aggregated data through 
TDS:   

How to Obtain a Time Series at a Single Point using TDS   

How to Obtain a Spatially Subsetted Time Series using TDS 

6.0 More Information 

Groundwater and Soil Moisture Conditions from GRACE Data Assimilation Site:  
https://nasagrace.unl.edu/ 

The Land Data Assimilation System (LDAS) Project:  https://ldas.gsfc.nasa.gov/ 

7.0 Acknowledgements 

12 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 README Document for GRACE Data Assimilation Version 3.0 Global Product 

The GRACE-DA-DM GL V3.0 data are produced by NASA GSFC Hydrological Sciences Laboratory 
(HSL). 

The GLDAS data are produced by the NASA GSFC Hydrological Sciences Laboratory (HSL). Please 
refer to Rodell et al. (2004) for more information about the GLDAS project.  

Development and production of the GRACE-DA products has been supported by NASA's 
Terrestrial Hydrology Program, NASA's Applied Science - Water Resources Program, and NASA's 
GRACE and GRACE-FO Science Team.
